# AGENTS.md — hello_world

Operating contract for GitHub Copilot, OpenAI Codex, and any other AI agents working in this repository. Read this file in full before making any change.

---

## 1. Project Overview

**Name:** hello_world

**What it does:** A minimal Flask REST API that provides personalised greetings and records them in an in-memory store. It exposes four REST endpoints (index `/`, health `/health`, greet `/greet/<name>`, and greetings `/greetings`) and has no external dependencies or persistent storage.

**Primary language:** Python (requires `>=3.11`)

**Framework:** Flask `>=2.3`

**Build/package tool:** pip via `pyproject.toml` (authoritative source for dependencies)

**Test framework:** pytest

**Entry point:** `app.py`

---

## 2. Commands

All commands below are derived from verified sources. Do not substitute or augment them unless the source files have changed.

### Install
```bash
pip install -e .
```
> Source: `pyproject.toml` — this is the authoritative install path. Do **not** edit `requirements.txt` for project dependencies; it is not the authoritative source.

### Build
```bash
pip install -e '.[dev]' 2>/dev/null || pip install -r requirements.txt
```
> Source: Makefile. Note: there is no `[dev]` extras section in `pyproject.toml`; the `2>/dev/null || ...` fallback to `requirements.txt` will always trigger. See Pitfalls §4.

### Run
```bash
python app.py
```
> Source: entry point is `app.py` (high confidence).

### Test
```bash
pytest
```
> Source: `pyproject.toml` `[tool.pytest]` section (high confidence). Do not add flags, paths, or plugins that are not already in `pyproject.toml`.

### Lint
```bash
ruff check .
```

### Format
```bash
ruff format .
```
> Source: `pyproject.toml` `[tool.ruff]`. Active rule sets: `["E", "F", "I"]`. Line length: `88`.

---

## 3. Safe Operations

The following operations are explicitly permitted for agents in this codebase:

- **Add new GET or POST endpoints** to `app.py` alongside the existing four routes.
- **Add new test functions** in `tests/test_app.py` to cover new or existing behaviour.
- **Update greeting message formatting** inside the `/greet/<name>` handler in `app.py`.
- **Add new utility modules** as standalone `.py` files at the repository root alongside `app.py` (not inside a new package unless `pyproject.toml` is updated accordingly).
- **Add new dependencies** by appending to the `dependencies` list in the `[project]` section of `pyproject.toml`. Do not add them only to `requirements.txt`.
- **Update `requirements.txt`** when new dependencies are added, to keep it consistent, but treat `pyproject.toml` as the source of truth.

---

## 4. Forbidden Operations

The following operations **must never be performed** without explicit human review and coordinated changes across every affected file:

- **Do not remove or rename any existing public endpoint** (`/`, `/health`, `/greet/<name>`, `/greetings`) without simultaneously updating all tests in `tests/test_app.py` that reference that endpoint.
- **Do not change the `_greetings` in-memory list structure** (its name, type, or element schema) without updating every consumer in `app.py` and every assertion in `tests/test_app.py` that reads from it.
- **Do not rename the Flask application variable `app`** in `app.py`. The test suite imports and uses this exact name to obtain the test client. Renaming it will silently break all tests.
- **Do not remove or modify pytest configuration** in `pyproject.toml` under `[tool.pytest]`.
- **Do not remove or modify ruff configuration** in `pyproject.toml` under `[tool.ruff]`.

**Restricted write paths:** Not determinable from source — fill in `agent-context.json` `static.restricted_write_paths` after reviewing your repo.

---

## 5. Domain Glossary

These terms are used throughout the codebase. Definitions are derived directly from the source code analysis.

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Greetings** | The in-memory store (`_greetings` list, `app.py` line 7) that holds all recorded personalised greetings. Intentionally simple — there is no database backing this list. All data is lost on process restart. |
| **Service Identity** | The root `GET /` endpoint that returns the service name and version string. Source: `app.py`. |

> The `/health` endpoint is an infrastructure probe and is not a domain concept.

---

## 6. Key Components

### `app` — `app.py`
The sole application module. Defines the Flask application instance (named `app`), registers all four REST endpoints, and owns the module-level `_greetings` list. This file is simultaneously the importable module and the runnable server entry point — importing it has side effects (see Pitfalls §2).

### `test_app` — `tests/test_app.py`
The complete test suite. Validates all API endpoints via Flask's built-in test client, which must be obtained from the `app` object defined in `app.py`. All agent-authored tests must live here or in new files under `tests/`.

---

## 7. Potential Pitfalls

This section is critical. Read every item before writing or modifying code.

---

### Pitfall 1 — Module-level mutable state causes test pollution

**What an agent will try to do:** Write multiple test functions that each call `POST /greet/<name>` (or the equivalent greet endpoint) and then assert on the contents of `GET /greetings`.

**Why it breaks things:** `_greetings` is a module-level list defined once when `app.py` is first imported. It is never reset between test functions. Each call to `/greet/<name>` appends to the same list for the entire pytest session. A test that asserts `len(greetings) == 1` will fail if any earlier test has already added entries, and the failure will be order-dependent and hard to diagnose.

**What to do instead:** Either re-create the Flask test client (and therefore re-import the module) for each test by using a `function`-scoped pytest fixture, or explicitly clear `_greetings` in a fixture teardown step. Do not write tests that assume `_greetings` starts empty unless isolation is guaranteed by the fixture.

---

### Pitfall 2 — Importing `app.py` triggers side effects

**What an agent will try to do:** Import symbols from `app.py` at module level in a utility script, a new test helper, or a management command (e.g. `from app import _greetings`).

**Why it breaks things:** `app.py` serves as both the Flask module and the runnable entry point. At import time it executes route registration and initialises `_greetings`. Any code that imports `app.py` will trigger these side effects immediately, which can interfere with test setup ordering, produce duplicate route registrations in edge cases, or cause unexpected behaviour in scripts that only wanted a single symbol.

**What to do instead:** Always import from `app` within a fixture or function scope, not at the top of a module. If you need to share symbols across multiple test files, expose them through a `conftest.py` fixture rather than bare module-level imports.

---

### Pitfall 3 — No database: data is ephemeral and not thread-safe

**What an agent will try to do:** Add a feature that relies on `_greetings` persisting across server restarts, or deploy the application behind a multi-worker server (e.g. gunicorn with multiple worker processes) and expect consistent greeting history.

**Why it breaks things:** There is no database. `_greetings` exists only in the memory of the running Python process. On any restart, all recorded greetings are gone. Under concurrent requests within a single process, Python's GIL does not guarantee atomicity for list operations at the application level, and across multiple worker processes each worker has its own isolated list.

**What to do instead:** Before adding any feature that requires durable or shared state, introduce a persistence layer (e.g. SQLite, Redis) and update `pyproject.toml` dependencies accordingly. Do not paper over this with module-level locks — replace the architecture instead.

---

### Pitfall 4 — `pip install -e '.[dev]'` always falls back to `requirements.txt`

**What an agent will try to do:** Run the build command (`pip install -e '.[dev]' 2>/dev/null || pip install -r requirements.txt`) and assume the `[dev]` extras (e.g. pytest, ruff) were installed from `pyproject.toml`.

**Why it breaks things:** There is no `extras_require` / `[project.optional-dependencies]` `dev` group in `pyproject.toml`. The `pip install -e '.[dev]'` command will fail silently (suppressed by `2>/dev/null`) and the fallback `pip install -r requirements.txt` will run instead. If `requirements.txt` is out of date or missing a dev tool, that tool will be absent without any visible error.

**What to do instead:** If you need a reproducible dev environment, add an `[project.optional-dependencies]` `dev` section to `pyproject.toml` listing `pytest`, `ruff`, and any other dev tools, then run `pip install -e '.[dev]'` without the fallback. Until that section exists, verify that `requirements.txt` contains all tools you need before relying on the build command.

---

### Pitfall 5 — Renaming the `app` variable in `app.py` silently breaks all tests

**What an agent will try to do:** Rename the Flask application instance in `app.py` from `app` to something more descriptive (e.g. `flask_app`, `application`, `hello_app`) as a style improvement or to avoid a name collision.

**Why it breaks things:** `tests/test_app.py` obtains the Flask test client by referencing the `app` name directly from `app.py`. Renaming `app` will cause an `AttributeError` or `ImportError` at test collection time. Because pytest collects all tests before running any, the failure will appear as a collection error rather than a test failure, which can be confusing to diagnose.

**What to do instead:** Do not rename `app`. If a name collision arises, resolve it by renaming the conflicting symbol instead. If a rename is truly necessary, update every reference in `tests/test_app.py` and any other file that imports `app` from `app.py` atomically in the same commit, and run `pytest` to verify before merging.

No secrets handling mechanism is configured in this repository. Establish one before adding any credentials.

## Irreversible Operations

No irreversible operations are present in this codebase. State changes are ephemeral (in-memory only).
